tech.headline: Longi develops 27.27% efficient heterojunction back-contact solar cell
tech.summary: Longi and Yangzhou University researchers developed a laser-based manufacturing strategy for HJT back-contact solar cells. This method mitigates shock-wave-induced damage, improving passivation stability and device performance. The approach resulted in a certified 27.27% efficient solar cell, providing an industrially viable pathway toward the fabrication of ultra-high-efficiency photovoltaic technology through improved laser processing techniques.

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels Power Both Sides
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight on the front and rear, boosting output without extra land. This technology helps meet rising demand for clean power while lowering the levelized cost of electricity, making solar more competitive in the global energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a mirror‑backed solar panel that absorbs light from above and reflects from below. A typical bifacial module can gain 10‑30% more energy than a monofacial one, especially when installed over reflective surfaces like sand or snow.
learn.c2_text: The rear side receives albedo light reflected from the ground. Albedo values range from 0.1 (grass) to 0.9 (white concrete). Higher albedo increases rear‑side generation, so mounting height and surface choice are key design parameters.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023 solar tender encouraged bifacial modules, estimating a 15% yield boost for desert projects in Rajasthan. The policy aims to add 30 GW of bifacial capacity by 2027, accelerating the nation’s 100 GW solar target.
learn.stat1: Global bifacial capacity reached 7 GW in 2022 (IRENA)
learn.stat2: Rear‑side gain can exceed 25% on white gravel
learn.stat3: India installed 12 GW bifacial modules in 2023

learn.title: Smart Grids & Advanced Metering
learn.intro: Smart grids integrate digital communication, sensors, and analytics to balance supply and demand in real time. Advanced metering empowers consumers and utilities to optimize energy use, a cornerstone for high‑penetration renewables and resilient power systems.
learn.c1_text: Think of the grid as a smartphone network: data flows both ways. Smart meters send usage data every 15 minutes, while utilities push price signals or load‑shedding commands, enabling dynamic response to renewable fluctuations.
learn.c2_text: Algorithms process meter data, weather forecasts, and storage status to dispatch resources within seconds. This reduces reliance on fossil peaker plants, cutting CO₂ by up to 5 Mt annually in Europe.
learn.c3_text: India’s Saubhagya scheme targets 250 million smart meters by 2025, linking over 40 GW of distributed solar. The rollout supports net‑metering and time‑of‑use tariffs, encouraging rooftop owners to shift consumption to daylight hours.
learn.stat2: Average demand response potential: 15% of peak load
learn.stat4: Grid losses reduced by 2‑3% with advanced metering

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms on Water
learn.intro: Floating photovoltaic (FPV) plants sit on reservoirs, lakes, and coastal ponds, turning water surfaces into power generators. They conserve land, reduce water evaporation, and can improve panel efficiency through cooling, offering a win‑win for renewable growth and water security.
learn.c1_text: Picture a lake covered with a solar carpet. FPV farms can generate 5‑10% more electricity than ground‑mounted systems because water cools the panels, increasing efficiency by about 0.5% per °C lower temperature.
learn.c2_text: Shading reduces surface heating, cutting evaporation by up to 70% on large reservoirs. This conserves billions of cubic meters of water annually, crucial for drought‑prone regions.
learn.c3_text: The 500 MW floating plant at Bhadra Reservoir (Karnataka) became India’s largest in 2023, saving an estimated 30 million m³ of water each year while feeding 800 GWh to the grid.
learn.stat2: Efficiency gain: up to 10% vs land‑based
learn.stat4: India: 1.5 GW floating solar under construction (2024)

learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering in India
learn.intro: Rooftop solar lets households and businesses generate clean electricity where it’s used. India’s net‑metering policy lets prosumers feed excess power back to the grid, receiving credit on their bills—accelerating the nation’s renewable ambition and energy independence.
learn.c1_text: Solar cells convert photons into electricity via the photovoltaic effect. A typical 5 kW rooftop system produces about 22 kWh per day in Delhi, enough to power a 4‑person household’s lighting, fans, and appliances.
learn.c2_text: When generation exceeds demand, the inverter pushes surplus to the grid. Utilities record the export and credit the consumer at the same tariff as consumption, effectively “spinning the meter backwards.”
learn.c3_text: MNRE’s 2022 guidelines standardize net‑metering across states, capping export at 10 % of contracted capacity. By March 2024, rooftop installations reached 25 GW, contributing 8% of India’s total solar capacity.
learn.stat2: Average rooftop yield: 4.5 kWh kW⁻¹ day⁻¹
learn.stat3: Net‑metering credits: 1 kWh export = 1 kWh import

learn.title: Electric Buses & Trucks Explained
learn.intro: Electrifying heavy‑duty transport cuts oil demand, lowers emissions, and reduces urban air pollution. Batteries and emerging hydrogen fuel cells power buses and trucks, reshaping logistics and public transit for a low‑carbon future.
learn.c1_text: An electric bus uses a large lithium‑ion pack (200‑400 kWh) delivering torque instantly. A 150 kWh pack can travel 250 km on a single charge, comparable to a diesel bus’s 300 km range but with zero tailpipe emissions.
learn.c2_text: Depot fast chargers (150 kW) can replenish 80% of a bus’s battery in 30 minutes. Opportunity charging at stops (30 kW) adds 30 km per 5‑minute pause, enabling continuous operation on busy routes.
learn.c3_text: The Delhi Metro ordered 300 electric buses under the FAME‑II scheme, targeting a 30% reduction in fleet emissions by 2027. Incentives include up to ₹2 crore per bus and tax exemptions for charging infrastructure.
learn.stat1: Global electric bus sales: 600 k units (2023)
learn.stat2: CO₂ saved: 0.5 t per bus‑km vs diesel

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells Break Efficiency
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells promise record‑high efficiencies and low‑cost manufacturing, potentially surpassing silicon’s performance ceiling. Their rapid progress could revolutionize photovoltaics, making solar power cheaper and more versatile for the energy transition.
learn.c1_text: These cells use a crystal lattice of organic‑inorganic halide materials that absorb a broad spectrum of sunlight. Lab prototypes reached 25.7% efficiency, rivaling top silicon cells, with thin‑film layers only a few hundred nanometers thick.
learn.c2_text: Stacking perovskite on top of silicon creates tandem cells, capturing high‑energy photons in the perovskite layer and lower‑energy photons in silicon. This architecture can push efficiencies beyond 30% in laboratory settings.
learn.c3_text: The Indian Institute of Technology Delhi launched a ₹150 crore perovskite hub in 2022, aiming for commercial‑scale modules by 2027. Government grants target 10 GW of perovskite‑based PV by 2035.
learn.stat3: Projected cost: $0.30 W⁻¹ for large‑scale production
